objective: Conduct a structured user interview analysis using real app store reviews and build an empathy map for a real Indian product.

realWorldAnchor.example: Meesho's pivot from social commerce for urban users to focusing on Tier 2/3 resellers — driven entirely by listening to users they didn't expect

tasks.title: The Mom Test — How to Talk to Users
tasks.summary: The Mom Test is a classic PM book that explains why most user interviews are useless — because people lie to be polite. It teaches you to ask about real behaviour, not hypothetical preferences. The core rule: never ask if someone would use your product. Ask about the last time they had the problem.
keyTakeaways: Ask about the past, not the future — 'Would you use this?' is useless
The best user interviews feel like casual conversations about their life, not pitches
Pay attention to what people do, not just what they say
Compliments and encouragement during an interview are red flags, not green ones

tasks.title: Empathy Mapping — The UX Tool PMs Underuse
tasks.summary: An empathy map is a simple 2x2 framework that captures what a user says, thinks, does, and feels. Nielsen Norman Group explains how to build one and why it forces product teams to move beyond demographics into real human motivation.
keyTakeaways: Empathy maps capture the gap between what users say and what they actually feel
The 'pains' and 'gains' quadrants are most useful for feature prioritisation
Build empathy maps from real data — interviews, reviews, support tickets — not assumptions
One empathy map per user segment, not one for all users combined

tasks.keyword: Meesho user research Tier 2 India product decisions
tasks.whyItMatters: Meesho is one of the best Indian examples of a product team that genuinely listened to users they didn't originally design for. Understanding how they discovered their real user base teaches you what good user research actually changes.
whatToLearn: How Meesho discovered their core user was a Tier 2 housewife, not an urban reseller
What product decisions changed after this insight
How Indian PM teams conduct user research differently from Western companies
Any blog posts or talks by Meesho PMs or founders about user discovery

tasks.title: Build a User Empathy Map from Real Reviews
tasks.promptTitle: Build a User Empathy Map from Real Reviews
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a PM coach working with Pranav, an APM candidate.

Today's exercise is about understanding users through real data.

Give Pranav these 4 Meesho app store reviews and ask him to build an empathy map:

Review 1 (⭐⭐⭐): "The app is good but the returns process is very confusing. I ordered a saree for my customer and the colour was different. Now I have to explain to her and I look bad. Please make returns simpler."

Review 2 (⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐): "I am a housewife in Nagpur. I started selling on Meesho 8 months back. Now I earn 12,000 per month from home. My husband was doubtful but now he also helps me pack orders. Very good app."

Review 3 (⭐⭐): "Catalogue is too slow to load on my 4G. I have Jio but in my area speed is not good. When I show products to customers in person the app takes too long. Lost 2 sales because of this."

Review 4 (⭐⭐⭐⭐): "Good for extra income. But I wish there was a way to track which customers bought what from me. I use a notebook now. Please add customer history feature."

Ask Pranav to identify:
1. What does this user SAY? (direct quotes)
2. What does this user THINK? (underlying beliefs)
3. What does this user DO? (actual behaviours)
4. What does this user FEEL? (emotions)

Then ask: based on this map, what is the single most important problem Meesho should solve first — and why?

Give him structured feedback on his empathy map and prioritisation reasoning. Score 1-10.

bonusTasks.title: How Swiggy Learned What Customers Actually Want
bonusTasks.summary: Swiggy's tech blog occasionally reveals their user research process — how they discovered that reliability (on-time delivery) mattered more than speed for their core users.
keyTakeaways: User research at scale uses quantitative signals to find the right qualitative questions
NPS scores told Swiggy what — user interviews told them why
Operational constraints (delivery partner behaviour) are user experience problems too

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Empathy map + problem statement for a CRED user
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Here are 5 real-style CRED user comments from Reddit and app store reviews:

Comment 1: "CRED is basically a rewards app now. I pay my credit card bills here because I get points. But I have no idea what to do with the points. They expire and I forget."

Comment 2: "Started using CRED because my CA told me to track credit card spends. The spend analysis is actually useful. But the app keeps pushing me to buy stuff I don't need."

Comment 3: "I have a 790 CIBIL score and I still got rejected for CRED Pay. What's the point of the score gating if the credit product doesn't work?"

Comment 4: "The UX is beautiful. But I feel like every new feature is trying to monetise me. Used to feel like a premium club, now feels like a shopping app."

Comment 5: "Only use it for bill payments. The gamification with coins is fun initially but after 6 months it feels hollow. I keep using it because it's convenient, not because it's rewarding."
assessmentTask.outputFormat:
1. An empathy map (Say / Think / Do / Feel) for the core CRED user based on these comments
2. One clear problem statement in the format: '[User] needs a way to [goal] because [insight]'
3. One product recommendation that addresses the problem statement
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM at a top Indian consumer startup, reviewing the Day 2 assessment of APM candidate Pranav.

He was given 5 CRED user comments and asked to build an empathy map, write a problem statement, and make a product recommendation.

Evaluate his submission:

**What I Got Right** — accuracy of empathy map, quality of problem statement, logic of recommendation
**What Needs Correction** — anything vague, generic, or misinterpreted
**Blind Spots** — what he missed that a strong PM would have caught
**India-Specific Note** — does he understand CRED's unique positioning in the Indian market?
**Open Points for Tomorrow** — one question to carry into Day 3 about Jobs To Be Done

Score: X/10
Reforge Level: Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform
